why: B correctly navigates System > Resource Provisioning with accurate provision levels and grounds its citations in the supplied context, while A invents a wrong navigation path (System > Configuration > Device Management) and C adds a slightly off Tomcat/memory note.
All three correctly admit the context lacks upgrade-path detail and defer to release notes, but B and C are tighter and cleaner than A's longer restatement.
A and B both explain HTTP::redirect accurately with clean examples and correctly cite K7910, while C's code example has a bracket-syntax error ([HTTP::uri] starts_with ...]) that would not parse.
All cite the plausible-but-not-in-context K000138933, but B and C add further uncited K-numbers (K02521182, K000091280 size claim) as fabrications, while A stays closest to the supplied references.
All three correctly identify RFC 3143 with the right URL; B's citation label reads slightly cleaner than A/C's awkward [Source: ...] formatting.

B and C correctly route to System > Provision with reasonable provision levels, while A invents nonexistent menus (System > Platform > Modules) and a fabricated db key (sys.provision.dns), making it wrong.
A gives accurate pre-upgrade checks and honestly defers to release notes, while B fabricates a specific Bug Tracker article number (K16790) and miscalls AskF5 as DevCentral (also in C).

C correctly documents the optional status argument (HTTP::redirect url [status]) and a clean example, A is solid, but B fabricates nonexistent flags (-code and -append_query_string), which are not valid HTTP::redirect options.
All three give reasonable, honestly-hedged failure causes (network, auth, size, config) without fabricating citations; A is the most concise and well-organized.
The answer is RFC 3143; A honestly admits it can't name a single RFC and points to related ones, B wrongly centers RFC 2068, and C fabricates a nonexistent RFC 2206 with an invented title.
